column4: BP control decreased by 4% to 80%; the downward trend and sub-target indicators need attention.
column5: Multiple indicators remain below target; current interventions are not fully addressing the drivers of sub-optimal performance at this facility.
column6: An overdue call rate of 81% meets the target; returned-to-care at 81% meets the 60% threshold and is sustaining patient re-engagement.
column7: Titration rate of 80% meets the target; however, statin prescribing at 75% and other sub-target indicators are limiting overall programme performance.
column8: Review the patient visit register for dropout patterns across this quarter.
column9: Confirm overdue call logs are being completed and actioned every workday.
column10: Check whether titration decisions are documented in patient records at each visit.

column4: All key indicators at or above target; BP control increased by 10% this month.
column5: BP control of 78% reflects consistent patient follow-up, adequate drug supply, and active clinical titration; this combination should be documented as a model for others.
column6: An overdue call rate of 95% ensures missed patients are rapidly reengaged, preventing the dropout that typically drives BP control decline over time.
column7: A fudging ratio of 1.6 is within the acceptable range, suggesting BP readings are recorded accurately and are not being inflated to improve apparent performance.
column8: Confirm the patient follow-up schedule is being consistently maintained each month.
column9: Verify the drug ordering process to understand how stock-outs have been prevented.
column10: Document specific practices at this facility so others in the district can replicate them.

column4: BP control decreased by 6% in one month; this sudden drop requires immediate investigation.
column5: A 6% single-month fall in BP control exceeds normal variation and indicates a discrete event such as a stock-out, staff absence, or a data recording error.
column6: Not-controlled BP worsened by 10% and missed visits improved by 4% this month; these together indicate whether the root cause is clinical or attendance-related.
column7: Titration rate of 61% and overdue call rate of 54% should be reviewed alongside the patient register to determine if patients attend but leave uncontrolled.
column8: Check the register for data entry errors in this month's BP readings.
column9: Confirm drug stock was continuously available throughout the reporting period.

column4: Drug stock at 14 days; patients attending now risk leaving without medication.
column5: With only 14 days of drugs in stock, patients seen this week may leave without medication, directly triggering adherence failure and a BP control decline within weeks.
column6: Low drug stock consistently predicts a sharp BP control fall within four to six weeks as previously adherent patients disengage from care once supply runs out.
column7: Titration rate of 61% and overdue call rate of 60% show whether clinical and follow-up capacity can recover quickly once stock is restored.
column8: Physically count drug stock and compare to register entries today.
column9: Confirm whether a supply order has been submitted this cycle.
column10: Ask staff whether any patients have been turned away due to stock shortages.

column4: BP control has remained at 46% for four or more consecutive months with no measurable improvement, despite persistent programme activity.

column6: An overdue call rate of 56% and returned-to-care rate of 28% have remained flat; patients are either not being reached or attending without effective clinical management.
column7: A titration rate of 41% that is not improving suggests clinicians may need additional support or supervision to consistently escalate treatment for uncontrolled patients.
column8: Ask the facility in-charge what specific barriers are preventing improvement.
column9: Review whether call logs show genuine contacts or recorded-but-not-reached attempts.
column10: Check whether titration protocols are displayed and accessible at the point of care.

column4: All key indicators at or above target; BP control increased by 8% this month.

column4: BP control has remained at 42% for four or more consecutive months with no measurable improvement, despite persistent programme activity.
column5: Stagnation at 42% BP control over four or more months indicates that current interventions are not sufficient to shift performance; a fundamentally different approach is needed.
column6: An overdue call rate of 27% and returned-to-care rate of 43% have remained flat; patients are either not being reached or attending without effective clinical management.
column7: A titration rate of 36% that is not improving suggests clinicians may need additional support or supervision to consistently escalate treatment for uncontrolled patients.
column8: Ask the facility in-charge what specific barriers are preventing improvement.
